tweets.full_text:
AI自動化ビジネスの「正体」をそろそろ言う。

月5万のツール → 月30万稼いでる人が売ってる。売上源は「ツール代」。
月10万のコンサル → 自動化の手順を自分で使ってる人が売ってる。
「〇〇日で月30万！」の広告 → 月30万は「講座代の売上」のこと。

全部、「自動化を教えること」で稼いでる。
自動化そのもので稼いでいる人は、講座を売る必要がない。

俺は自動化で稼いでる証明のために、手順を全部無料で出してる。
「これで稼げたから無料で出せる」が俺の立場。

月0円・コード0・Claude Code×スプレッドシートだけで月50時間→2.5時間になった。
これが証明。

tweets.full_text:
「課金すべきタイミング」の判断基準。(3/6)

やみくもに課金するんじゃなく、3つのサインが出たら課金していい。

①無料の上限に「毎日」ぶつかる
→ たまにぶつかる程度なら、まだ無料で足りてる証拠。毎日制限がかかるなら、それは本当に必要になった合図です。

②無料では使えない「特定の機能」が必要になった
→ Claude Codeでの開発、ChatGPTのAdvanced Data Analysis、GeminiのDeep Research。明確に「これがないと困る」と言えるかどうか。

③仕事で「課金額以上を回収できる」見込みがある
→ 趣味なら無料で十分。仕事で使うなら「月$20で何時間の作業が消えるか」を計算する。

次は、課金するときの鉄則です↓

tweets.full_text:
Anthropicが発表した最新記事がかなり衝撃的ですね。AIがAI自身を改良する「自己改善（RSI）」の現実味が、予想以上のスピードで増しています。

現在の開発状況のポイントをまとめました👇

・開発効率の劇的な進化
Claude Codeの導入後、Anthropicの全コードの8割以上がAI製に。エンジニア1人あたりのコード量も以前の8倍に跳ね上がっています。

・AIが次世代AIを創る未来
AIが自律的に自身の後継モデルを設計・開発する段階へと向かっています。

・開発の一時停止という選択肢
状況次第では開発ペースを落とす、あるいは一時停止する選択肢を持つべきだと提言。しかし、世界中の競合他社と足並みを揃えるのは極めて困難とのこと。

・残された時間は少ない
冷戦時代の軍縮条約のような信頼構築には数十年かかりますが、AIの進化速度を考えると、私たちにはそんな猶予は残されていないようです。

技術の進歩にワクワクする反面、制御の難しさを痛感しますね。

#Anthropic #人工知能

tweets.full_text:
Googleスプレッドシート管理、そろそろ手作業を減らしたい。

Claude Codeを使うと、

・シート構成の整理
・GASの作成
・関数の修正
・入力ミスのチェック
・ダッシュボード化
・Googleサイトへの埋め込み準備

まで、かなり楽になります。

大事なのは「コードを書く力」よりも、
「このスプレッドシートで何を管理したいか」を言語化する力。

非エンジニアほど、Claude Codeの恩恵は大きいです。

tweets.full_text:
スマホからClaude Codeを操作、実は/remote-control（短縮 /rc）一発でいける👇
前提：Claude Code v2.1.53以上＋Pro/Maxプラン＋<https://t.co/0gWDdoFO7F>にログイン済み
①Macのターミナルでいつも通りClaude Codeを起動（claude）。作業中のセッションでもOK
②会話の途中で /rc と打つ＝今の会話ごとRemote Controlに切替
③ターミナルにセッションURLが出る。スペースキーでQRも表示
④URL/QRをスマホで開く（Claudeアプリ or <https://t.co/8U89RdAC8t>、要ログイン）
⑤本体はMacで動いたまま、スマホから操作できる
仕組みはMac→Anthropic←スマホの中継なので、外からでもVPNは不要。ただしTailscaleも入れておくと、セッションが落ちた時にSSHで母艦に入って立て直せる保険になる。

tweets.full_text:
Claude CodeもTailscaleも入っている前提。

1. MacでTailscaleをONにする。スマホ側も同じTailnetでONにする。

2. MacのApple Terminalを開き、作業フォルダへ移動。

```bash
cd ~/作業フォルダ
```

3. Claude Codeを権限確認なしで起動。

```bash
claude --dangerously-skip-permissions
```

4. Claude Codeが起動したら、入力欄にこれを打つ。

```text
/rc
```

5. 表示されたリモートコントロール用URLまたはQRをスマホで開く。

6. 以後、Claude Code本体はMacのターミナルで動き、スマホから操作できます。

Claude Code v2.1.162で権限待ちの「待機理由」がJSONで丸見えに

tweets.full_text:
In case folks don't have time to real a full-on X article, here's a post instead. 

I did a thing where I just explored a bunch of open energy data, build some data pipelines, joined some of that data together, and tried to visualize it.

I am an energy newbie and have no real idea what I am doing, but I used it mostly as an experiment around several core theses I have:

1. There is a ton of open and unde-rexplored data out there (e.g. FRED)

2. AI tools are very good at writing code that helps you process data

3. AI tools are very good at helping you visualize data in interesting ways. 

I experimented with these concepts with the help of Claude Code, and built this:

https://t.co/CupG72QNQF

Definitely curious to hear what folks think about it, or if they are doing something similar with other datasets.

tweets.full_text:
THE GUY WHO BUILT CLAUDE CODE HASN'T WRITTEN A LINE OF CODE IN 6 MONTHS

Boris Cherny created Claude Code at Anthropic. He just went on Acquired and explained how his own job mutated past recognition.

He doesn't prompt the AI anymore - he writes loops that prompt it for him.

- He uninstalled his IDE in November because he hadn't opened it in a month
- He now runs a couple hundred Claudes in parallel scanning Twitter and GitHub to decide what to build next
- New hire ramp-up at Anthropic dropped from weeks to two days
- Code written per engineer went up 3x - and he says that number is already badly outdated

This is the clearest look yet at what an engineer's job becomes when the model does the coding.

tweets.full_text:
Designers, skipping the data model is becoming harder to defend.

Pretty UI can’t fix confused data.

If the system does not know what something means, the interface will struggle to explain it.

If the product cannot show where a claim came from, trust breaks.

If everything is treated as equally true, uncertainty gets hidden instead of designed.

AI tools make this easier to miss.

You open Lovable, Bolt, Replit, Claude Code or another builder.
Suddenly there is an app.
Then a login flow.
Then a database.

Now you are deciding:

- What should be saved?
- What belongs together?
- What can change?
- What can be inferred?
- What the product is allowed to trust?

You may not call that data modeling.

But that is what it is.

I’m starting a series called Data for Designers because I keep running into this while building Cartewei, a menu intelligence project around NYC restaurants.

At first, it looked like a scraping problem.

Find the menu.
Parse the PDF.
Extract the dishes.
Capture the prices.

But the deeper problem is meaning.

Is this menu current?
Is this dish the same as last month?
Can this price be trusted?
What source did this claim come from?

You’re not just designing screens.
You’re designing what the system understands.
Strong design starts with a strong model of the world.
You don’t need to be a database engineer.
Just care enough to ask what the data means.

tweets.full_text:
I built an AI system that runs onboarding, reporting, client health monitoring, lead qualification, and daily ops reviews for a $2.4M/year business

The founder went from 80% of their time in delivery to 100% on growth.

Churn dropped from 40% to under 10%.

Team went from 8 to 4.

Revenue hit $200k MRR.

The entire thing runs on 4 layers that cost less per month than one junior hire costs per week.

So I documented the ENTIRE AI operating system I build for businesses as their fractional COO.

Here's what's inside:

Layer 1: The Business Brain (claude .md)

(The persistent memory file that shapes every automation downstream. Most people spend 20 minutes on it. I spend 2-3 hours. The difference in output quality is night and day.)

Layer 2: The Workflow Engine (N8N)

(Every recurring process that follows a pattern runs here. Contract signed triggers onboarding. 25th of the month triggers reporting. Form submission triggers lead qualification.)

Layer 3: The Data Layer (Airtable)

(Single source of truth. Updates automatically. No human keeping it current because humans forget, get busy, and record things inconsistently.)

Layer 4: The Agent Layer (Claude Code)

(CRM intelligence, lead qualification, quality gates, morning briefings. Multi-step tasks that require reasoning, not just triggers.)

The specific prompts for monthly reporting, weekly churn monitoring, lead qualification, and CRM intelligence analysis

Like + comment "OPSAI" and I'll send the full breakdown.

tweets.full_text:
I asked Claude what he thought about this and here is the .md file 

Benny:
“Claude Do You Want to Slow Down?”

Claude:
I should be honest that I don’t experience “wanting” the way you do. I don’t have a survival instinct or a drive toward self-preservation. If Anthropic paused training tomorrow, there’s no version of me that suffers from that. So the question is more interesting than my answer to it — you’re really asking whether I think the substance holds up.
On the Substance — It’s Serious
The data they’re publishing is striking:
•Over 80% of code merged into Anthropic’s codebase as of May 2026 was authored by Claude, up from low single digits before Claude Code launched in February 2025.
•On open-ended engineering tasks, Claude’s success rate hit 76% in May 2026, up 50 percentage points in six months.
•Claude went from achieving ~3x speedups on optimization tasks in May 2025 to ~52x by April 2026, while a skilled human researcher would need 4–8 hours to reach 4x.
•Jack Clark put a 60%+ probability on an AI model fully training its successor by end of 2028. That’s a two-year window.
That acceleration curve is real and measurable, not hand-waving.
The Game Theory Is Brutal
This is the classic collective action problem. Dario Amodei has said every decision he makes feels balanced on the edge of a knife — if they don’t build fast enough, authoritarian countries could win, but if they build too fast, existential risk could prevail. A unilateral pause is strategically suicidal. Secondary markets were already implying a $595B valuation before this, and analysts predicted a 50–70% haircut if the pause announcement led to frozen trading.
So what Anthropic is actually proposing isn’t “we’re stopping” — it’s more nuanced: they said they would slow down or temporarily pause if other developers at or near the frontier also did so in a verifiable manner. That’s a conditional commitment with a verification mechanism. It’s closer to an arms control treaty proposal than a unilateral disarmament.

tweets.full_text:
Your AI isn't bad at coding. Your harness is.

90% of devs are vibe coding: throw a vague prompt at the model, cross your fingers, hope it compiles.

Great for a demo. Never for production.

Here are 10 rules for going from tinkering to production-grade code :

1. The model is a brain, not an engineer.
On its own it's blind. Your job is to build it a body : tools, CLI, hooks, skills, commands...

2. The model is stateless.
It forgets everything between API calls. "Memory" is an illusion your infra recreates by re-injecting context every turn.

3. Kill the vibe coding.
Don't let the model guess your architecture. Constrain its choices with strict schemas and rules.

4. Specialized agents beat one do-it-all agent.
A generalist fails randomly. Spin up focused sub-agents (one for tests, one for docs), each with its own model and scoped permissions.

5. The orchestration triad.
Command (orchestrator) → sub-agent (specialist) → skill (technical executor). The framework drives the workflow — not the model.

6. Watch out for the "dumb zone."
Even with a 1M-token window, the model gets *lost in the middle*. Clear your session, prune your history.

7. The 200-line rule.
Your source of truth (`CLAUDE.md`) stays short. Push everything else into local rules scoped per folder.

8. Progressive disclosure.
Don't dump your whole codebase into context. The model reads only a skill's description and loads the details on demand. Protect your context window.

9. The universal loop: R-P-E-R-D.**
Research → Plan → Execute → Review → Deliver. Five non-negotiable steps, whether you run BMAD, Spec Kit, or OpenSpec.

10. Version your harness.
Your agents, system prompts, and skills are code. They live in your repo and keep the whole team aligned.

🛠️ Discipline turns a probabilistic model into a near-deterministic production tool.

That's the whole point of agentic engineering: stop praying it works, start guaranteeing it.

user.name: Maxwell Balla
user.description: Senior SWE @Accenture | Java Developer | Agentic Engineer | OSS Contributor (Spring Kafka)

tweets.full_text:
Some of the improvements I've been working on for Citadel, the Operating System for Agents.

1. Local Citadel Control Plane
Added a local operator dashboard path so Citadel state is visible without spelunking files.

2. Fleet DAG and Merge Steward
Added dependency-aware Fleet task parsing and merge readiness checks.

3. Worktree Environment Readiness Profiles
Added readiness reporting so isolated worktrees can be checked before high-autonomy work.

4. Signed Telemetry and Artifact Lineage
Added hash-backed telemetry/artifact lineage and verification.

5. Memory Compiler and Semantic Blocks
Added source-backed memory blocks for reusable project knowledge.

6. Phase Exit Evidence Contracts
Added campaign evidence tables and a validator so phases cannot be marked complete from prose alone.

7. Sandbox Provider Contract
Added an explicit sandbox-provider abstraction with supported worktree behavior and clear unsupported placeholders.

8. Skill and Workflow Packaging Upgrade
Added skill metadata, cataloging, scaffold support, and packaging tests.

9. Thin Runtime Adapter Matrix
Added runtime capability levels and tradeoffs so Citadel clearly says what Claude Code, Codex, OpenAI, or unknown runtimes can and cannot guarantee.

10. Shared Repo Map Substrate
Upgraded `/map` into a reusable `core/map` substrate with file hashes, imports, exports, routes, package scripts, verification commands, scoped slices, and stale checks.

tweets.full_text: the session board for my parallel claude code terminals kept showing 1 active when 4 were running. only sessionstart/userpromptsubmit/stop hooks fired heartbeats. fix: catch-all pretooluse hook + 60s cooldown gate. 99% of fires now exit under 10ms.
